title: Feeding laying hens through the seasons
subtitle: A year of feed adjustments that keep birds in lay and condition.
excerpt: Laying feed in February is not laying feed in August. The protein needs shift with daylight hours and weather, and the supplements that matter in winter are different from the ones that matter in moult. A round-the-year feeding plan for a back-garden flock.
sourceNotes: DEFRA welfare code for laying hens. Smallholder Magazine feed columns. Lewis Wright, The Practical Poultry Keeper, Internet Archive. AHDB poultry feeding factsheets.

glossaryTerms.definition: The standard commercial complete feed for laying hens, formulated at about sixteen per cent protein with the calcium and minerals a laying bird needs. Sold by every UK feed merchant.

Hard insoluble stone the bird swallows to grind food in the gizzard. Distinct from oyster shell, which is calcium for eggshell formation. Hens kept on hard surfaces need grit offered separately; free-ranging hens often pick up enough from the soil.

The annual feather change, usually in autumn, during which a hen drops old feathers and grows new ones. Laying pauses through the moult because the bird's protein goes into feather growth rather than egg production.

recipeTools.notes: Treadle feeder keeps rats out and waste down.

content.text: A back-garden flock kept on
layers' pellets

all year is well-fed, but it is fed without nuance. Hens have peaks and dips through the year that suit small adjustments. The right feed mix in February gets a flock back into lay early; the right mix in October keeps body weight on through the moult and means strong birds going into winter.
The base layer all year

The bulk of the feed at every season is a complete layers' pellet or mash, free-fed in a treadle feeder out of the rain. A laying hen eats about a hundred and twenty grams a day; six birds get through one kilo a day, around seven kilos a week. Buying in twenty-kilo sacks works for a small flock, gives roughly three weeks of feed, and sees the sack used before the feed goes stale. Store in a metal bin with a tight lid against rats.

Pellets or mash? Pellets keep the coop tidier because the birds cannot scratch them out; mash gives the birds a longer eating time and reduces feather pecking in confined flocks. A small free-ranging flock does fine on pellets; a flock confined for any length of time benefits from mash. Both are fed the same way.
Spring: building back into full lay

From late February as daylight stretches, hens that came out of lay over winter start back. A small handful of mealworms or a tablespoon of soya flakes scattered in the run mid-morning gives a protein lift that hastens the first eggs of the year by a week or two. Spring grass is rich in carotenoids and turns yolks deep orange within a fortnight of birds getting out on fresh ground. Worm the flock in March if not done at the autumn end.
Summer: keeping condition through hot weather

Hens eat less in hot weather. A finishing hen in a July heatwave can drop her intake by fifteen per cent and her lay rate with it. Feed early and late in the day, leave the trough in the shade, and offer cold snacks: a halved cucumber, a watermelon rind, frozen sweetcorn kernels dropped in a bowl of water. The snacks are mostly water and welcome to a bird shedding heat. Eggshell quality holds best when
oyster shell grit
is offered free choice in a separate small feeder; the birds pick it up as they need it.
Autumn: moult and condition
From September the flock starts to
moult

. Laying slows or stops altogether through October and November while the birds grow new feathers. A small handful of sunflower seeds, hemp seed, or scrambled egg added once a week gives the protein for feather growth without spoiling the base feed. A bird with a tired patchy back at the end of October needs more time; she is not laying because her body is rebuilding. Do not cull a moulting layer; she comes back into lay by February or March with full plumage and a year ahead of her.
Winter: warmth, water, and reduced expectation

Hens need more feed in cold weather to maintain body temperature. Increase the daily portion by ten to fifteen per cent from December through February, or simply leave the feeder fuller. A warm-water bran mash on a frosty morning is an old practice that still works: warm water, bran, a handful of mixed corn, scrambled to a porridge, served in a shallow bowl. The birds eat it eagerly and the warmth carries them through to the noon ration. Water is the bigger winter risk than feed; check the drinker is not frozen at first light and at the last round.

Mixed corn in the run an hour before roost is a winter habit worth keeping. The birds bed down on a full crop, the corn digests overnight and generates metabolic heat. Use it as a supplement to the layers' pellets, not a replacement; corn alone is too low in protein for laying hens.
Extras and kitchen scraps

The UK welfare code forbids feeding kitchen scraps that have passed through a domestic kitchen, on the basis of disease control. The rule is widely ignored on smallholdings but worth knowing. Garden waste from outside the kitchen, surplus eggs scrambled and cooled, weeds pulled from the vegetable patch, and bolted lettuce are all fine. Avoid mouldy bread, salty or oily leftovers, and anything from the allium family in large quantity.
What you might have found

attrs.heading: Feed troubleshooting
attrs.intro: Lay rate, feather condition, and feed intake are the three things to track.

items.symptom: Eggshells suddenly thin or shell-less in a previously healthy flock.
items.cause: Calcium short. The flock is laying faster than the calcium intake supports.
items.fix: Top up the oyster shell grit feeder; refill if it has been empty for a week. Add a tablespoon of crushed eggshell to the kitchen scrap if you are giving any. The shells firm up within ten days.

items.symptom: Feather pecking in a confined winter flock.
items.fix: Hang a cabbage from string in the run so the birds peck at a swinging object. Add a tablespoon of mealworm or scrambled egg to the morning feed. Check stocking density; a flock with less than three square metres per bird in the run pecks more.

items.symptom: Lay rate dropped from five eggs a day to one.
items.cause: Most likely a moult or seasonal day-length drop, but worth checking for fox stress, broody hens, or one bird hiding eggs.
items.fix: Look for feather drop and new feather pins. If it is a moult, ride it out and feed for condition. If the birds look fine, check for hidden nest sites in the run.

items.symptom: Feed gone in a day and birds clamouring.
items.cause: Rats are taking the bulk overnight, or the treadle feeder is open at the wrong tension.
items.fix: Set the treadle weight so a rat cannot trip the lid. Lift the feeder off the ground overnight. Look for rat runs in the bedding and tackle the colony before the problem grows.

content.text: Hens do not need expensive feeds or supplements. A clean layers' pellet, free-choice grit and shell, fresh water that never freezes solid, and a small treat tied to the season is the whole programme. Read the birds first and the bag last.
